card.intuition: Explore as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. Mark nodes visited to avoid cycles. Works on both directed and undirected graphs using a stack (recursive call stack or explicit).
card.analogy: Exploring a cave system by always taking the leftmost unexplored tunnel until you hit a dead end, then backtracking to the last junction and taking the next tunnel.
card.time_complexity: O(V + E) — each vertex and edge visited once
card.space_complexity: O(V) for visited set + O(V) recursion stack
when_to_use: Topological sort, cycle detection, SCC (with finish-time tracking)
Generating all paths or permutations via backtracking
gotchas: Always mark a node visited BEFORE recursing into it — not after — to handle back-edges correctly
For disconnected graphs, loop over all vertices as potential DFS roots
Recursive DFS may hit Python's recursion limit on large graphs — use an explicit stack if n > 10^4

exercise.prompt: Implement iterative DFS and return the visited order.
exercise.starter_code:
def dfs(graph, start):
    """
    graph: adjacency list dict {node: [neighbours]}
    Returns list of nodes in DFS visit order.
    """
    visited = set()
    order   = []
    stack   = [start]
    while stack:
        node = stack.pop()
        if node in visited:
            continue
        visited.add(node)
        order.append(node)
        # Push neighbours so the first neighbour is explored first
        for nbr in reversed(___________):   # reverse to preserve left-to-right order
            if nbr not in visited:
                stack.append(nbr)
    return order
hints: Iterate over graph[node] to get the neighbours of the current node; reverse them before pushing so the first neighbour ends up on top of the stack
